summary: O'Neil announces that high school students from Occupy Summer School have self-organized into an after-school activism group called 'Project Occupy' at their school. The students formed an organizing committee and plan weekly topic-based meetings covering issues like cultural appropriation. O'Neil expresses pride in the students' initiative and reflects on what adults learned from them about effective, generous protest tactics. The students aspire to eventually host events and create media content around issues they're passionate about.
key_points: High school students independently organized a continuing activism program after a summer educational initiative
The group will hold weekly topic-based meetings with readings and videos as material
Students taught adult organizers about keeping protests 'fun and generous' with symbolic gifts
Future plans include events, podcasts, and YouTube content around social issues
money_quotes: They are, as always, very sharp and observant. Teenagers are the best.
I told them how much they've taught us about keeping protests fun and generous, and giving away gifts of food or balloons (actually, both) to make their point well-received.
key_insight: Youth activism succeeds when young people are given educational foundations and then trusted to self-organize around issues that matter to them.
